title: Eliminating Code Duplication in Go with go:generate: A Practical Guide
subtitle: When building data-intensive applications in Go, you often find yourself writing repetitive boilerplate code.
excerpt: When building data-intensive applications in Go, you often find yourself writing repetitive boilerplate code.

content.html: When building data-intensive applications in Go, you often find yourself writing repetitive boilerplate code. A common scenario is handling multiple data transformation layers:

items: Converting GraphQL filters to database query parameters
Converting database records to API response models
Maintaining count queries with identical filter logic

content.html: This creates a maintenance nightmare. Every time you add a new field to your domain model, you must update three or more places in your codebase. The logic is identical — only the struct types differ.
Consider this pattern:

items: A filter conversion function that maps GraphQL filter inputs to database query parameters for list operations
An identical conversion function for count operations (same logic, different parameter struct)
A model conversion function that transforms database records to GraphQL response models

content.html:
That’s 50% duplication before you even write real business logic.
The Traditional Solutions and Their Limitations

content.alt: Eliminating Code Duplication in Go with go:generate: A Practical Guide

content.html: The simplest approach, but:

items: Maintenance becomes exponential as your domain grows
Bugs fixed in one place need fixing in multiple places
New developers don’t understand why the code is repeated
Refactoring becomes a multi-hour task

content.html: Reflection-based mappers like mapstructure or custom reflection utilities:

content.code: // Slow and loses type safetyresult, _ := mapstructure.Decode(source, &destination)

content.html: Drawbacks:

items: Runtime overhead (reflection is slow)
Loss of compile-time type checking
Harder to debug
Performance issues at scale
Complex error handling

content.html: Create an interface that multiple structs implement:
Drawbacks:

items: Still requires massive switch statements in each implementation
No reduction in boilerplate — just reorganization
Difficult to add new field types without changing the interface
Still a lot of manual code to write and maintain

content.html: Go’s built-in code generation tool provides the perfect balance between maintainability, performance, and type safety. Instead of fighting duplication, you generate the repetitive code from a single source of truth.
Go includes a go:generate A command that runs arbitrary tools during the build process. The typical workflow:

items: Define your data model in code (your source of truth)
Write a generator program that reads this model
Run go generate to create type-safe, optimized code
The generated code is committed to version control (optional but recommended)

content.html: Why this is better:

items: ✅ Zero runtime overhead — generated code is optimized at compile time
✅ Type-safe — full IDE support, compile-time checking
✅ Single source of truth — maintain mapping in one place
✅ Version control friendly — generated code is plain Go
✅ Easy to review — generated code is human-readable
✅ No magic — explicit, transparent transformation

content.html: Create a generator tool that contains your data transformation definitions:

content.code:
type FieldMapping struct {
    FilterName
    string // Name in the filter input
    SqlcFieldOut string // Name in SQL parameter struct
    FieldType string // "string", "int64", "float64", "time"
    MinField string // For range queries
    MaxField string // For range queries
}
type ModelFieldMapping struct {
    DBField string // Name in database record
    ModelField string // Name in API response model
    FieldType
    string
    ConvertFunc string // Conversion
    function to apply
}

content.html: Instead of duplicating field mappings across multiple functions, list them once:
This is your single source of truth. All transformation logic derives from these definitions.
Use Go’s text/template package to generate the boilerplate:

content.code:
const template =
`// Code generated; DO NOT EDIT.package servicefunc (s *Service) GraphqlFilterToListParams(filter *model.Filter) sqlc.ListParams {    params := sqlc.ListParams{}        if filter != nil {{{- range .FilterFields}}{{- if eq .FieldType "string"}}        params.{{.SqlcFieldOut}} = utils.ToNullString(filter.{{.FilterName}}){{- else if eq .FieldType "time"}}        params.{{.SqlcFieldOut}} = utils.ToNullTime(filter.{{.FilterName}})        params.{{.MaxField}} = utils.ToNullTime(filter.{{.MaxField}}){{- end}}{{- end}}    }    return params}`

content.text: Step 4: Wire It Into Your Build

content.html: Add a go:generate directive to your service file:
Run the generator:
This creates optimized, type-safe code automatically.

content.code:
Service.go├── graphqlFilterToListProductParams()     [200 lines]├──
graphqlFilterToCountProductParams()    [195 lines - 95% duplicate]└── dbGsmToGraphQLProduct()
[150 lines]

content.html: Every change requires updating multiple functions.

content.code:
Generator└── fields = []FieldMapping{...}     [1 definition]└── modelFields =
[]ModelFieldMapping{...}Generated:└── filter_mapper.gen.go              [auto-generated from single
source]    ├── GraphqlFilterToListProductParams()    ├── GraphqlFilterToCountProductParams()    └──
dbGsmToGraphQLProduct()

content.html: Add a field once, regenerate, done.
Adding a new field:

items: Before: Edit 3+ functions, increase the chance of bugs
After: Add one line to your configuration, run go generate

content.html: Fixing a conversion bug:

items: Before: Fix in all places it appears
After: Fix in the generator template, regenerate

content.html: Changing field mapping rules:

items: Before: Manual refactor across multiple functions
After: Update the configuration, regenerate

content.html: Code review:

items: Before: Review duplicate logic in multiple places
After: Review the generator template once

content.html: The generated code is indistinguishable from handwritten code:
This compiles to identical machine code as handwritten code. Zero overhead.
Use generators for boilerplate that follow predictable patterns. Don’t generate business logic.
Your generator tool should be ~100–200 lines. If it’s getting complex, you might be over-engineering.
Commit generated files to version control so:

items: Others can see what code is generated
CI can verify that the generation is up-to-date
Bisecting git history works correctly

content.html: Add a check to verify the generated code is current:

content.code: # Generate codego generate ./...# Verify no files changedgit diff --exit-code

content.html: Add comments to your field definitions explaining any non-obvious mappings:
Code generation isn’t new or exotic. Go’s go:generate makes it accessible for everyday problems. When you find yourself copying and pasting code patterns, consider:

items: Is this pattern repetitive?
Will it need to change frequently?
Does it follow a predictable structure?

content.html: If yes to all three, generate it.
